topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an aggressive, irritating buzz, mirroring the frantic rhythm in Sarah’s chest. She stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before her eyes. It was a simple comparison, yet it felt like a physical blow. Mark, who had just finished his quarterly review, had casually mentioned his salary package to a group of colleagues, and suddenly, Sarah was holding a very specific, very uncomfortable truth. Her fingers, gripping the mouse so tightly her knuckles were white, hovered over the keyboard. Every keystroke felt monumental, a step closer to some inevitable, unwelcome confrontation. She could feel a flush creeping up her neck, a hot, prickly sensation spreading through her skin. She needed to look busy, needed to appear perfectly composed, but her stomach was churning, a tight knot of dread refusing to loosen.

[story 2]

Liam traced the outline of the projection on his monitor, the blue light reflecting in his wide, darting eyes. He reread the email—a brief, factual notification that confirmed his worst fear. The silence in his small cubicle felt deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in his ears. He shifted in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. He took a deep, shallow breath, trying to steady the tremor running through his hands. He felt a distinct pressure building behind his sternum, a tightness that made swallowing difficult. He kept glancing at the door, half-expecting someone to walk in, ready to dissect the situation. He just wanted the moment to pass, to dissolve into the beige walls, before he had to face the inevitable awkwardness.

[story 3]

Maria smoothed down her blouse for the tenth time, a purely reflexive gesture designed to hide the slight tremble in her hands. The data was undeniable, starkly laid out on the screen, and it had landed with the force of a physical impact. A cold sweat beaded on her forehead, trickling down her temples. She started tapping her pen rapidly against the desk blotter, a nervous, repetitive motion that seemed to generate all the energy she possessed. Every sound seemed amplified in the quiet afternoon. She forced herself to stand up, needing to move, needing to escape the immediate vicinity of the numbers, but her legs felt unsteady, as if wading through thick water. She focused intensely on a distant water cooler, willing her breathing to slow, trying to keep the internal storm from boiling over.
